content.text: In React, data flows in a single direction: from parent components down to their children through props. A child component cannot directly modify its parent's state; it can only read what it is given. This constraint is not a limitation — it is the architectural property that makes React UIs predictable and debuggable.
. State lives somewhere up the component tree; components below receive it as props and render accordingly. When something needs to change, a child calls a callback function (passed as a prop) to
a state change from the owner — it never mutates state directly.

attrs.alt: Component tree showing props flowing downward from App through Sidebar and PostFeed to leaf components, and callbacks flowing upward

's state updater, which triggers a re-render and new props flow down again.

Why This Constraint Makes UIs Predictable
When you need to understand why a component rendered the way it did, you only need to look
the tree to find where its props come from. There are no hidden side-channels where a grandchild might reach up and mutate a grandparent's state.
Given the same props and state, a component always renders the same output. You can reproduce any UI state by providing the same data.
When a bug appears — "why did this button disappear?" — you trace the prop that controls it back to the state that owns it. The path is always top-down.
A change in one branch of the component tree cannot cause a sibling branch to re-render unexpectedly, because siblings don't share state (unless lifted to a common ancestor).
A component function with a given set of props is pure from the perspective of its render output. Tests simply pass props and assert on the rendered result.
Events Travel Up, Data Travels Down
The natural complement to downward data flow is upward event notification. A child does not mutate — it signals. The parent decides whether and how to update state.
(updating the input's controlled value) and a new
. The cycle is clear and predictable.

When two sibling components need to share state, that state cannot live in either sibling — it must "lift" to their nearest common ancestor. Lifting state is the direct consequence of one-way flow: shared data must always be owned by a component that can send it down to all interested parties.

Lifting state as high as the nearest common ancestor (and no higher) keeps the data source close to the components that use it. Lifting all the way to the root unnecessarily triggers more re-renders and makes the component tree harder to follow.
Props Are Read-Only — React Enforces This
React does not technically prevent you from mutating a prop in JavaScript, but doing so causes bugs that are extremely hard to trace:

Mutating an object prop (like pushing to an array) modifies the parent's state object directly. React does not detect this mutation because it uses reference equality for change detection — the reference hasn't changed, so it skips the re-render, leaving the UI out of sync with the underlying data.
Prop Drilling and When to Address It
A natural consequence of one-way flow is
— passing props through intermediate components that don't use them, just to deliver data to a deeply nested consumer.

Light drilling (2–3 levels) is perfectly fine and often the cleanest solution. At greater depths, React Context or a state management library makes more sense. The rule of thumb: reach for Context when a prop would need to pass through more than two components that don't actually need it.

Understanding the flow also explains React's re-render behaviour. When a component's state changes, React re-renders that component and — by default — all its descendants. Props are the channel through which a parent communicates the change.
Because props flow top-down, React can reason about what needs to update: anything downstream of a changed state is a candidate for re-render.
lets you opt specific subtrees out of unnecessary re-renders by comparing their props.

Frameworks like Angular and older Vue versions use two-way data binding: a UI element can both read from and write to a model variable. React deliberately avoids this in favour of explicit event handlers.
Harder — model can be mutated from many places
Easy — only the state owner calls setState
Can be hard — who changed the model?
Clear — grep for setState or dispatch
React's extra boilerplate is intentional. The explicit
handler is the visible record that "this UI element updates this piece of state". In two-way binding, that connection is implicit in a directive or macro — visible only if you know where to look.
One-Way Flow with Context and Redux
React Context and libraries like Redux extend one-way flow to wider trees without prop drilling, but they preserve the same fundamental direction:
a provider at the top of the tree publishes a value; consumers anywhere below read it. Consumers cannot modify it directly — they call a function exposed by the context (which calls

a store holds state; components read from the store (data flows down); components dispatch actions or call setters (events flow up to the store). The store updates and re-delivers data to subscribers — still strictly one direction.
When building a feature, apply these questions to keep data flow clean:
The component that owns state should be the lowest common ancestor of all components that need it.
Is the child trying to mutate a prop?
Replace mutation with an event callback. The parent decides how state changes.
If a prop passes through 3+ levels without being used, consider Context.
Is the component pure given its props?
A component that renders identically for the same props is easy to test, easy to memo, and easy to reason about.
One-Way Flow and the Flux / Redux Pattern
React's one-way data flow inspired the Flux architecture (2014) and later Redux, which formalise the pattern for application-wide state. The cycle follows three steps:
— a plain object describing what happened (
and returns new state, without mutations.
— components that read from the store re-render with the new data. Data flows down again.
The reducer is a pure function — identical input always produces identical output. Combined with actions as plain objects, this makes every state transition a
that you can log, replay, or undo — the foundation of Redux DevTools' time-travel debugging.

One-way flow and immutability are paired concepts. React's change detection relies on reference equality: if the object reference hasn't changed, React assumes the data hasn't changed. Mutations break this assumption.

Immutable updates are a direct consequence of one-way flow: because state is owned by one component and flows down as read-only props, state changes must produce new values rather than modifying old ones in place.

A shopping cart is a common example that ties together lifting state, one-way flow, and callback events. The cart state lives in the page component; every child that needs to read or modify it receives exactly what it needs — no more.
cannot see the cart — it only receives
. Every modification is a request to
, which makes the actual state change.
One-way flow gives you a deterministic path to any bug:
Find the component showing wrong data.
Open React DevTools, inspect its props.
Trace upward to the parent that passes them. Inspect its state.
Find the state updater (setState, dispatch) and add a log or breakpoint before it.
Fix the event handler that calls it. The path from UI bug to root cause is always top-down.
Compare this with a two-way binding scenario: when a model value is wrong, it could have been written by any number of view elements bound to it — there is no obvious starting point.
A component never mutates its own props — it signals the owner, which updates state.
State shared between siblings must be lifted to their nearest common ancestor.
One-way flow makes UIs reproducible, traceable, and testable.
Context and state managers extend the pattern to wider trees without breaking its direction.
